planting.note: Scarify seeds by nicking with a file or soaking in warm water for 24 hours before sowing; direct sow in well-drained, lean, sandy, slightly acidic soil after last frost. Atlantic pigeonwings dies back to the ground after frost in zone 7–8 and re-emerges from deep roots in spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Atlantic pigeonwings is a native twining vine of open woodlands, sandy roadsides, and pine flatwoods across the Southeast, closely related to the tropical culinary butterfly pea (C. ternatea) but hardier and better adapted to North American conditions. The large, powder-blue to lavender-blue pea flowers are inverted on the plant — the keel points upward rather than downward as in most legumes — with a distinctive white and yellow central mark. The tubular flowers are an important nectar source for bumblebees and sphinx moths that are physically capable of reaching the nectar. At woodland edges and in partial shade gardens, it provides a delicate, trailing structure on fences and shrubs throughout summer. Dies back to persistent roots in winter and re-emerges reliably in spring in zones 7–10. An outstanding native vining legume for the dry, sandy soils typical of the Gulf Coastal Plain.

planting.note: Scarify seeds and direct sow in spring in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; panicled tick trefoil grows naturally in dry to moderately moist open woodlands, woodland edges, and old fields throughout eastern North America.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun at woodland edges and in naturalized plantings; panicled tick trefoil is one of the most important native wildflower legumes of the eastern woodland edge — a tall, erect perennial with trifoliate leaves and loose, elongated panicles of small pink to lavender pea flowers in late summer. The plant is critically important as a host plant for Eastern tailed-blue, silver-spotted skipper, and several other butterfly and moth species that are specialists of native legumes. The jointed seed pods break into small, sticky segments (the 'ticks' that give the genus its name) and cling tenaciously to clothing and animal fur — an effective dispersal strategy that makes some people view the plant as a nuisance. In naturalistic gardens and wildlife corridors, it is an outstanding wildflower that seeds itself through open, lightly shaded areas without becoming aggressive in well-maintained beds.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in very well-drained, lean, sandy or rocky, acidic to slightly acid soil in full sun; sidebeak pencilflower is adapted to nutrient-poor, well-drained, acidic sandy soils and will decline in heavy, moist, or fertilized conditions.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, sandy, acidic soil in full sun; sidebeak pencilflower is a diminutive native perennial legume of dry, sandy pinelands, sandhills, and rocky acidic outcrops across the southeastern Coastal Plain. The genus name (Stylosanthes = 'stalk-flower') refers to the unusual structure of the flower, in which the petals are carried on a long, narrow floral tube or 'beak.' The tiny yellow flowers are produced throughout summer and fall, providing pollen and nectar for small native bees. This is one of the few leguminous plants native to the nutrient-poor sandy soils of the longleaf pine ecosystem, where it fixes nitrogen under conditions that would challenge most legumes. The wiry, semi-prostrate stems form a low, spreading mat that can serve as a drought-tolerant groundcover in dry, acidic, sandy conditions. Grows naturally in the same habitats as gopher tortoises in the Southeast.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in lean, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; trailing wild bean is an annual that self-sows readily and will maintain itself in sandy, open areas year after year without replanting. Scarify seeds for faster germination.

directions: Direct sow in lean, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade; trailing wild bean is a native annual vine of sandy beaches, dunes, open woodlands, and disturbed sandy soils across eastern North America. The small, pink to purple pea flowers, produced throughout summer, are visited by numerous native bees including specialist legume bees. The small, slender bean pods that follow are consumed by quail, turkey, and other ground-feeding birds. As an annual, it must re-establish from seed each year, but it self-sows readily in sandy, disturbed soils to form persistent colonies. Trailing wild bean is a nitrogen-fixing native annual that provides quick cover on sandy, disturbed sites while enriching soil fertility — making it useful for sandy slope stabilization and as a component of native meadow mixes. The twining stems climb through low shrubs and grasses to create a naturalistic, interconnected low-growing plant community.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; sensitive partridge pea self-sows prolifically in appropriate open, disturbed conditions and naturalizes readily in sandy roadsides, old fields, and sunny groundcover beds.

directions: Direct sow in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; sensitive partridge pea is a smaller relative of the showy partridge pea (C. fasciculata) that shares the same remarkable leaf sensitivity — the pinnate, compound leaves immediately fold closed when touched or disturbed, re-opening within minutes. The bright yellow flowers, smaller than partridge pea but produced in similar abundance, provide pollen for a number of native bees including specialist Chamaecrista bees that depend specifically on the pea flowers. It grows in the driest, most disturbed sandy and rocky soils across eastern North America, where it is one of the pioneering native annuals that colonizes bare, sunny ground. The seeds are consumed by quail and other ground-feeding birds. An easy self-seeding native annual for adding nitrogen fixation and wildlife value to dry, sunny, sandy garden areas with minimal intervention.
